lines.speaker: Rain
lines.text: Let's name what the crisis actually is. Because I think a lot of people hear "AI and education" and assume this is a conversation about cheating on homework.

lines.speaker: Flow
lines.text: Right, and it's — way bigger than that. The paper's claim is that the existential justification for modern schooling has collapsed.

lines.speaker: Flow
lines.text: For about a century, we told kids — and ourselves — that you go to school to be prepared for a job. You learn content, you pass tests, you get a credential, the credential gets you employment. The quiet deal under the whole factory model.

lines.speaker: Rain
lines.text: And AI can now do a lot of that cognitive work.

lines.speaker: Flow
lines.text: At professional levels. Writing, analysis, synthesis, even some forms of judgment. So when a teacher says to a student — learn this, you'll need it for your career — the student can reasonably say, "Will I?"

lines.speaker: Rain
lines.text: There's a stat in the paper that lands hard. As of 2024, an estimated 52% of online content is AI-generated. That shift happened in about two years.

lines.speaker: Flow
lines.text: So students are not just navigating a world where AI might take some jobs. They are already navigating an information environment where most of what they encounter wasn't created by humans.

lines.speaker: Rain
lines.text: And the responses from schools have been — uneven. A lot of bans.

lines.speaker: Flow
lines.text: Which the paper calls counterproductive. If the point of education is to prepare learners to navigate the world they're actually in, an AI-free classroom is preparing them for a world that doesn't exist. [sighs] You cannot build judgment inside a protected bubble and expect it to survive contact with the unprotected one.

lines.speaker: Rain
lines.text: So the crisis has a shape. The old justification — content mastery for job prep — doesn't hold. The world students will actually live in is saturated with synthetic content personalized to manipulate them.

lines.speaker: Flow
lines.text: And the question flips. It stops being "how do we prepare workers?" and becomes "how do we prepare thinking humans?"

lines.speaker: Rain
lines.text: Which is — [short pause] a better mission, honestly, than job preparation ever was.

lines.speaker: Flow
lines.text: That's the paper's line, almost word for word. The tragedy is that it required existential threat to get us to ask it.
